sourceMovie.title: A Million Ways to Die in the West
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit A Million Ways to Die in the West for the same reason it broke through in 2014: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a western rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Seth MacFarlane keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The comedy and western blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: ¡Three Amigos!
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: ¡Three Amigos! (1986) belongs to the same conversation as A Million Ways to Die in the West — both anchor their comedy in western emotion that doesn't let go. The funny undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: City Slickers
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If A Million Ways to Die in the West's comedy stayed with you, City Slickers (1991) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The western through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Moana
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where A Million Ways to Die in the West excels in comedy, Moana (2016) doubles down on adventure to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Madagascar: Escape 2 Africa
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Madagascar: Escape 2 Africa (2008) is the natural next step after A Million Ways to Die in the West: same comedy DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and adventure elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Elf
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of A Million Ways to Die in the West who respond to its comedy will find Elf (2003) immediately familiar. Both films share a family sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Spies in Disguise
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason A Million Ways to Die in the West fans keep landing on Spies in Disguise (2019) — the comedy is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Zack and Miri Make a Porno
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Zack and Miri Make a Porno draws from the same comedy well as A Million Ways to Die in the West, channeling romance in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2008 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Abominable
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Abominable (2019) captures the comedy appeal of A Million Ways to Die in the West without simply imitating it. The family streak running through both films is what puts Abominable at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: The Magnificent Seven
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The western appeal of A Million Ways to Die in the West — that tense edge, the action — translates cleanly into The Magnificent Seven (1960). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: The Quick and the Dead
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind The Quick and the Dead (1995) clearly understood what makes western work — the same quality that keeps A Million Ways to Die in the West rewatchable. Expect the same bittersweet rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

faq.question: What should I watch after A Million Ways to Die in the West?
faq.answer: Try ¡Three Amigos!, City Slickers, Moana—each shares DNA with A Million Ways to Die in the West while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is A Million Ways to Die in the West?
faq.answer: A Million Ways to Die in the West (2014) is best described as Comedy, Western. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is A Million Ways to Die in the West worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.1/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this comedy lane.
